channel_name: The Prof G Show · Scott Galloway
video_title: OpenAI’s First Acquisition: How TBPN Built a $30M Media Business
gist: TBPN founders John Coogan and Jordi Hayes explain how they bootstrapped a 17-month-old daily tech show into a ~$30M-revenue business that became OpenAI's first-ever acquisition (reportedly low-hundreds-of-millions, a 20x+ media multiple), built on a pro-advertising thesis and a 'Formula 1 sponsorship' ad model targeting only ~200,000 enterprise decision-makers. Along the way they trade market calls on Meta/gambling, Bending Spoons' IPO, Snap, the Anthropic-vs-OpenAI race, and SoftBank.

insights.claim: TBPN generated ~$5M in ad revenue in 2025 with 11 employees, is tracking past $30M in 2026, and is bootstrapped, profitable, and had taken zero outside capital while only 17 months old at acquisition.
insights.speaker: Scott Galloway
insights.so_what: A benchmark for how lean, high-ARPU trade media can out-monetize far larger general audiences; template for niche B2B content businesses.
insights.quote: 11 employees uh five million in ad revenue in 2025. You're tracking past uh from what I see here 30 million in 2026 bootstrapped profitable zero outside capital show was 17 months old at acquisition.

insights.claim: TBPN earns ~50% more revenue than Prof G (~$20M in 2026) despite far fewer downloads and views (~70,000 viewers/episode across platforms, 58,000 YouTube subscribers), showing revenue-per-viewer beats raw audience scale.
insights.speaker: Scott Galloway
insights.so_what: Audience quality and monetization model, not size, drive media P&L; investors should discount raw download/subscriber counts.
insights.quote: you guys have 50% more revenue and my So, now granted, I'm the idiot reading Zip Recruiter ads

insights.claim: TBPN deliberately makes content for a maximum addressable audience of ~200,000 people worldwide—operators and investors who deploy tens of billions annually—rather than chasing mass reach.
insights.speaker: Jordi Hayes
insights.so_what: Deep-niche 'trade media' can command enterprise ad budgets; a reusable model for monetizing high-net-worth decision-maker attention.
insights.quote: we believe that there's 200,000 people in the world max that we make content for and these people are running businesses

insights.claim: TBPN sells advertising only on annual fixed-rate contracts, giving advertisers free incremental impressions as the show grows while giving TBPN predictable revenue to hire and invest ahead of growth.
insights.speaker: Jordi Hayes
insights.so_what: Fixed annual deals de-risk a small team's cash flow and align incentives; a structural alternative to volatile CPM ad sales.
insights.quote: we only sold advertising on an annual basis... they'd be on a fixed rate. So, if we grew beyond what we were projecting, it was just like free incremental impressions

insights.claim: TBPN pitches ad deals as sponsoring a Formula 1 team—one integrated contract covering logos on merch, clips, IRL events and screen placement—rather than selling per-impression CPM ad insertions.
insights.speaker: Jordi Hayes
insights.so_what: Escaping the CPM game lets premium creators capture enterprise-scale sponsorship dollars; a monetization playbook for B2B media.
insights.quote: I was pitching advertising with us as like sponsoring a Formula 1 team.

insights.claim: For enterprise/SaaS advertisers, closing a single large customer (e.g., one public company for Ramp) can pay for an entire annual ad deal, making CPM-based pricing economically inferior for such products.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: Explains why B2B ad inventory should be priced on customer value, not impressions—relevant to creator-economy and ad-tech valuations.
insights.quote: if you're advertising enterprise products, one customer can pay for the entire ad like placement, the entire deal

insights.claim: TBPN prioritizes enterprise advertisers over consumer ones because an audience member can spend $5M/year on SaaS or $50M on inference but can only buy one or two consumer products like an Eight Sleep.
insights.speaker: Jordi Hayes
insights.so_what: Ad-inventory allocation should follow buyer wallet size; frames why B2B media commands premium rates over consumer.
insights.quote: they can spend $50 million on on inference, but they can only buy like one aid sleep, right? Or maybe two.

insights.claim: TBPN replaced long 90-second ad reads with roughly 250 twenty-second reads per deal, betting on repetition and frequency to keep live viewers from bailing.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: Short high-frequency ad formats may retain audiences better in live/long-form video—useful for ad-supported streaming and podcast monetization.
insights.quote: instead of giving you a smaller number of 90-cond ads, we'll give you, you know, 250 20 second ads.

insights.claim: TBPN monetizes clips by embedding advertiser logos and a full ad read at the end of every one-minute clip, so even a few-thousand-view clip reaches the exact niche buyers as de facto trade media.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: Clip monetization and precise audience targeting can outperform broad view counts; a lever most media companies leave unused.
insights.quote: all of our clips have brands on them, logos, and then at the end of every one minute clip is a full ad read

insights.claim: OpenAI made TBPN its first-ever media acquisition, reportedly for the low hundreds of millions—an unprecedented ~20x+ revenue multiple for a podcast under two years old, far above normal media multiples.
insights.speaker: Scott Galloway
insights.so_what: Signals AI labs will pay premium multiples for owned marketing/distribution assets; watch for more lab acquisitions of media/creator brands.
insights.quote: the rumor is you guys got sold for the low hundreds of millions... call it a 20x plus revenue multiple which media businesses never trade at.

insights.claim: TBPN argues OpenAI must build an ad-supported free tier because most American households will cancel even a $10/month product, making $20/month subscriptions insufficient for mass adoption.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: Predicts ads are coming to ChatGPT; a structural shift with large implications for OpenAI's revenue model and ad-market TAM.
insights.quote: you guys think that everyone in the world can spend $20 a month on on software... most American households will like cancel a $10 a month streaming product

insights.claim: OpenAI acquired TBPN primarily for marketing help—billboards and Super Bowl ads—because the founders had run viral pro-OpenAI stunts (Super Bowl ad, a mock 'Claude with ads' product) that OpenAI itself couldn't run.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: Reveals the strategic rationale (owned marketing voice) behind AI-lab media M&A; frames the deal as brand/marketing, not content.
insights.quote: this was the deal that made the most sense to to help on the marketing side and that includes things from billboards to the next Super Bowl ad.

insights.claim: TBPN contends AI labs' fear-based marketing to raise capital has backfired, leaving public opinion scared of AI despite everyday usefulness, and that labs need product-benefit messaging ('where's our Steve Jobs').
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: A shift from doom-marketing to use-case marketing could reshape AI adoption curves and public/regulatory sentiment—watch labs' messaging.
insights.quote: this sort of like fear-based approach to selling the product is going to just go really really really badly and it has

insights.claim: Galloway predicts Meta will directly copy prediction-market/gambling products like Kalshi and Polymarket, using its ~2-billion-user distribution to capture value, driven purely by shareholder-value incentives.
insights.speaker: Scott Galloway
insights.so_what: If Meta enters real-money prediction markets, it threatens standalone platforms (Kalshi/Polymarket) and adds a new revenue vector—watch for launch.
insights.quote: he looks at calcium poly market and says, I know I'm going to take a two billion person hose and fire it at this... So I think it'll be a direct ripoff.

insights.claim: Galloway forecasts that post-World Cup data will show more money was wagered on World Cup games via Kalshi/Polymarket than has ever been wagered on any event in history.
insights.speaker: Scott Galloway
insights.so_what: Would validate prediction markets as a massive new betting category and a takeover target for big platforms—watch wagering volume disclosures.
insights.quote: I think we're going to find out post World Cup that there was more money wagered on World Cup games on calcium poly market than have ever been wagered on anything before in history.

insights.claim: Coogan argues Meta is undervalued—worth ~$3 trillion—but will carry a permanent discount because of the metaverse and AI overhangs, and would be worth more if Zuckerberg simply stayed focused on the core ad business.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: A focus-vs-diversification valuation debate on Meta; the 'permanent discount' framing is a watch item for M[META] holders.
insights.quote: I personally think Meta is like a a $3 trillion company and is going to permanently have a discount on it because of the metaverse, because of AI.

insights.claim: TBPN's simplest explanation for Meta's product moves is that it is culturally in Meta's DNA to launch a version of any hot consumer thing regardless of harm—'the largest second mouse in history' alongside Apple.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: A predictive lens: expect Meta to fast-follow whatever consumer category is hot (gambling, video, AI)—useful for anticipating competitive threats.
insights.quote: They're the they're the largest second mouse in history, maybe beside Apple.

insights.claim: Bending Spoons (AOL, Vimeo, WeTransfer owner) is pricing its IPO led by JPMorgan and Goldman, having swung from ~$270M revenue and a $120M loss in Q1 2025 to ~$625M revenue and ~$27M profit in Q1 2026, implying a ~$2.5B run rate.
insights.speaker: Scott Galloway
insights.so_what: Concrete financials for an imminent IPO; the rapid pivot to profitability is the key underwriting variable to scrutinize.
insights.quote: Q1 2025 was uh about 270 million and they lost 120 million Q126 they're now at 625 and they pivoted to profitability like 27 million.

insights.claim: Bending Spoons is valued at roughly $18-20B (~8x revenue) with 88% recurring revenue but only ~13% organic growth—mostly a roll-up—and carries high leverage at ~4x debt.
insights.speaker: Scott Galloway
insights.so_what: Bull case is a cheap SaaS roll-up of durable brands; bear case is heavy leverage and thin organic growth—central to the IPO trade.
insights.quote: the bare case is that it's highly levered. It's four it's its debt is four times which is pretty large leverage.

insights.claim: Bending Spoons' edge is buying legacy brands and running them with cheap young Italian labor to cut costs, since salaries in Napoli are far below US levels.
insights.speaker: Scott Galloway
insights.so_what: Labor-arbitrage plus AI efficiency is the margin thesis behind the roll-up; a repeatable model for acquiring and optimizing mature software brands.
insights.quote: salaries are much cheaper in Italy than in the US if you buy an American

insights.claim: Coogan argues legacy brands like AOL and Vimeo are AI-resilient because, having survived cloud and mobile disruption and built on advertiser relationships, they are unlikely to be cloned by vibe-coding competitors that mainly threaten hot young startups.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: Inverts the usual 'AI kills incumbents' view—durable low-growth cash cows may be safer than high-growth targets; relevant to the Bending Spoons bull case.
insights.quote: they've already faced disruption from cloud, disruption from mobile, disruption from AI is probably not going to hurt those companies either because these are very resilient.

insights.claim: Galloway is considering buying Snap, arguing the biggest tech-market unlock would be Snap spinning out its Spectacles Group, given Snap's ~$7B market cap against ~$6B+ expected revenue this year ($1.5B in Q1) and a stock down 93% in five years.
insights.speaker: Scott Galloway
insights.so_what: A concrete long/catalyst idea for SNAP: a Spectacles spin-out could unlock a claimed ~80% valuation discount—watch for spin-out news.
insights.quote: I think the biggest unlock in the tech market right now would be if Snap spuned Spectacles Group.

insights.claim: Coogan argues Snap remains defensible despite its collapse because it retains zero marginal cost and network effects, so even a vibe-coded direct competitor cannot replicate its liquidity pool of content.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: Network-effect moat supports a Snap floor thesis even as AI lowers app-building costs—relevant to the SNAP risk/reward.
insights.quote: still zero marginal cost and network effect. So even if someone vibe codes a direct competitor, it's not going to have the liquidity pool of content.

insights.claim: Galloway calls Anthropic's overtaking of OpenAI the most vicious number-two-to-number-one move he has ever seen in the corporate world, happening in roughly 90 days rather than over years like Apple vs Microsoft.
insights.speaker: Scott Galloway
insights.so_what: Frames the AI-lab leadership race as unusually fast and fluid; competitive position can flip quickly—relevant to lab valuations and funding.
insights.quote: this has been the most vicious number two to number one I've ever seen in the corporate world.

insights.claim: Coogan attributes Anthropic's rise over OpenAI to the value of focus and notes AI demand is so large that even the seventh-ranked codegen startup is 'crushing it' and raising at multi-billion valuations.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: Demand for intelligence is deep enough to fund many winners; 'focus beats complacency' is the founder lesson—watch codegen funding rounds.
insights.quote: the number seven codegen startup is like crushing it and raising it like a billion multi-billion dollar valuation because because like there's just so much demand

insights.claim: SoftBank's Masayoshi Son argues in a new deck that SoftBank is undervalued because the market prices its 'eggs' (portfolio stakes) but not the 'goose' itself—and his 2019 slide predicting AI traffic would drive the stock up has since coincided with a ~5x rise.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: SoftBank's NAV-discount 'goose vs eggs' argument plus Son's prescient AI call is a watch item for the stock's re-rating narrative.
insights.quote: what matters is not the eggs, it is the goose itself... he said it was because of AI traffic that's why it's going to go up.

insights.claim: Galloway frames Masayoshi Son as effectively a capital-repatriation conduit—channeling Gulf oil capital (PIF, Mubadala) into US entrepreneurs via SoftBank's aggressive AI bets.
insights.speaker: Scott Galloway
insights.so_what: A provocative lens on SoftBank as a vehicle moving sovereign Gulf capital into US tech—context for who ultimately funds AI buildout.
insights.quote: he's an agent of the CIA charged with reallocating capital or repatriating capital back from the Gulf.

insights.claim: TBPN's early traction came from format innovation—two tech insiders talking through 30-50 topics per 90-minute show for 150 hours before doing any guest interviews—claiming a monopoly on that format.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: Differentiated format plus relentless marketing, not celebrity guests, drove the flywheel—a repeatable creator go-to-market lesson.
insights.quote: there was no one else that had us talking about crazy ideas for 90 minutes. We had a monopoly on that.

insights.claim: TBPN's 'love letter' distribution tactic—clipping their 4K-filmed reactions to individuals' social posts and quote-tweeting the originals—converted roughly 50 people per day into engaged followers early on.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: A concrete do-things-that-don't-scale growth loop for creators; personalized high-production engagement beats broadcast posting.
insights.quote: if someone quotes your post with a thoughtful commentary that's filmed in 4K with cinema cameras and the hosts are wearing suits and they print it... it's just wow.

insights.claim: Coogan argues social-media platforms face a long-term shareholder-value flywheel risk: kids get addicted, the next generation of parents bans the apps for their own kids, eroding future users.
insights.speaker: John Coogan
insights.so_what: A terminal-value bear case for social platforms rooted in generational backlash—an under-modeled long-duration risk for M[META] and peers.
insights.quote: kids get addicted to social media, the next generation doesn't let their kids use the platforms... then that destroys shareholder value.
